name: ngrep
title: ngrep
description:
grep for network traffic
ngrep strives to provide most of GNU grep's common features,
applying them to the network layer.  ngrep is a pcap-aware tool that
will allow you to specify extended regular expressions to match
against data payloads of packets.  It currently recognizes TCP, UDP
and ICMP across Ethernet, PPP, SLIP and null interfaces, and
understands bpf filter logic in the same fashion as more common
packet sniffing tools, such as tcpdump and snoop.

arguments.description: Colorize matches in ASCII output.
Show empty packets. Normally empty packets are discarded because they have no payload
arguments.raw: -e  Show empty packets. Normally empty packets are discarded because they have no payload

arguments.description: Display help/usage information.
Ignore case for the regex expression.
arguments.raw: -i  Ignore case for the regex expression.

arguments.description: Make stdout line buffered.
Don't put the interface into promiscuous mode.
arguments.raw: -p  Don't put the interface into promiscuous mode.

arguments.description: Be quiet; don't output any information other than packet headers and their payloads (if
arguments.raw: -q  Be quiet; don't output any information other than packet headers and their payloads (if

arguments.description: Do not try to drop privileges to the DROPPRIVS_USER.
arguments.raw: -R  Do not try to drop privileges to the DROPPRIVS_USER.

arguments.description: a timestamp in the form of +S.UUUUUU, indicating the delta between packet
arguments.raw: -T  a timestamp in the form of +S.UUUUUU, indicating the delta between packet

arguments.description: Print a timestamp in the form of YYYY/MM/DD HH:MM:SS.UUUUUU everytime a packet is
arguments.raw: -t  Print a timestamp in the form of YYYY/MM/DD HH:MM:SS.UUUUUU everytime a packet is

arguments.description: Display version information.
Invert the match; only display packets that don't match.
arguments.raw: -v  Invert the match; only display packets that don't match.

arguments.description: Match the regex expression as a word.
arguments.raw: -w  Match the regex expression as a word.

arguments.description: the match expression as a hexadecimal string. See the explanation of match ex-
arguments.raw: -X  the match expression as a hexadecimal string. See the explanation of match ex-

arguments.description: Dump packet contents as hexadecimal as well as ASCII.
arguments.raw: -x  Dump packet contents as hexadecimal as well as ASCII.

command_examples: ngrep <-hNXViwqpevxlDtTRMC> <-IO pcap_dump > < -n num > < -d dev > < -A num > < -s snaplen > <
use_cases: When you need Kali Linux, use ngrep because grep for network traffic.
When the engagement is in the install phase, use ngrep as a cataloged Kali Linux tool with operator ACCEPT/CANCEL gating.
When you need a concrete invocation, run `ngrep <-hNXViwqpevxlDtTRMC> <-IO pcap_dump > < -n num > < -d dev > < -A num > < -s snaplen > <` as documented upstream for ngrep.
When chaining Kali Linux tools, pair ngrep with sibling catalog entries sharing its attack_surface/phase_hint.
When validating lab setup, run `ngrep --help` (or the README entrypoint) and confirm required hardware/deps from the catalog.

documentation.how_to_use: Install/setup: sudo apt install ngrep. Run the primary entrypoint as shown in upstream docs, e.g. `ngrep <-hNXViwqpevxlDtTRMC> <-IO pcap_dump > < -n num > < -d dev > < -A num > < -s snaplen > <`.
